title: PL/I Basics
description: Learn the fundamentals of PL/I programming.

theory.markdown:
## PL/I Basics: getting the program shape right

### What PL/I was designed for
PL/I was designed as a broad, general-purpose language for IBM systems, with features intended to cover both commercial and technical workloads. Historically, PL/I’s design goals and trade-offs are well documented in the programming languages literature, and modern Enterprise PL/I implementations continue that lineage on z/OS.

### The minimal structure of a runnable program
Most standalone examples begin with a main procedure:
- `PROC OPTIONS(MAIN);` declares the entry point.
- declarations (`DCL`) define storage and types.
- statements perform computation.
- output is written.
- `END <name>;` closes the procedure.

### Declarations (DCL) as a discipline
PL/I is explicit about data description. A declaration gives a name and a type/attribute set (for example, integer-like fixed binary or character strings). This matters because your later correctness and debugging depend on **type + length** being consistent.

### Output: a simple, common baseline
A straightforward way to print values in beginner programs is `PUT SKIP LIST(...)`. It’s not the only mechanism, but it’s a useful baseline because it keeps focus on program structure and data.

---

### References (academic/official)
1. IBM, *Enterprise PL/I for z/OS Programming Guide* (program structure, compilation, and programming model). `https://www.ibm.com/docs/en/SSY2V3_6.1/pdf/pg.pdf`
2. George Radin, *The early history and characteristics of PL/I*, ACM SIGPLAN Notices (1978). `https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/960118.808389`
theory.content:
Welcome to PL/I Basics

PL/I, short for Programming Language One, is a procedural programming language that was designed to support business, scientific, engineering, and system programming. In this chapter, you begin with the absolute foundations: how a PL/I program is structured, how variables are declared, how values are assigned, and how output is displayed.

The goal of this chapter is not to overwhelm you with advanced syntax, but to make sure you understand the essential pieces required to read and write a very small program. Once you know how a PL/I program starts, how it stores data, and how it prints results, you have the base needed for every later topic.

How a Basic PL/I Program Starts

A standalone PL/I program commonly starts with PROC OPTIONS(MAIN). This tells PL/I that the procedure is the main entry point of the program. At the end of the program, you use an END statement, usually with the procedure name, to close it clearly.

So a simple structure often looks like this: start the procedure, declare variables, write statements that do the work, display the results, and then end the procedure. This clear structure is important because it makes PL/I programs easier to read and organize from the beginning.

Declaring Variables with DCL

PL/I uses DCL, short for declare, to define variables. A declaration gives the variable a name and a data type. For example, FIXED BIN(31) is commonly used for whole numbers, while CHAR(n) is used for text.

In the exercise for this chapter, you need numeric variables, so you will mainly use declarations such as DCL X FIXED BIN(31);. This tells the program that X stores an integer value. A similar declaration can be used for a second number and for a third variable that stores the total.

Assigning Values and Doing Arithmetic

Once a variable is declared, you can assign a value to it with the = operator. For example, X = 10; stores the value 10 in the variable X. PL/I supports standard arithmetic operators such as +, -, *, and /.

For this chapter's exercise, the most important operation is addition. You will take two numeric variables, add them together, and store the result in a third variable. That means the theory you need is very direct: declare the variables, assign values, calculate X + Y, and save that result in something like TOTAL.

Displaying Output

A common beginner-friendly way to display output in PL/I is with PUT SKIP LIST. The PUT statement writes output, SKIP moves to a new line, and LIST prints values in a simple readable format. For example, PUT SKIP LIST('SUM =', TOTAL); will display text together with the computed value.

This is exactly what you need for the exercise. After computing the sum, you print the result so the user can see the final value. That completes the basic cycle of a beginner program: input by assignment, processing with arithmetic, and output with PUT SKIP LIST.

What You Need for the Exercise

  • Start the program with PROC OPTIONS(MAIN)
  • Declare numeric variables with DCL and FIXED BIN(31)
  • Assign values using =
  • Calculate the sum with +
  • Display the result with PUT SKIP LIST
keyConcepts: Basic numeric and character data types
Basic output with PUT SKIP LIST

exercise.instructions: Write a PL/I program that declares two numeric variables, assigns values to them, calculates their sum, and displays the result.
exercise.hint: Use DCL for declarations, = for assignments, and PUT SKIP LIST to print the final value.

questions.question: What is DCL used for in PL/I?
Which statement is commonly used to print output in beginner PL/I programs?
Which data type is suitable for storing whole numbers?
What does PROC OPTIONS(MAIN) indicate?
options: The procedure is the main program

questions.question: Which operator is used to assign a value to a variable?

theory.markdown:
## Core concepts: arrays + iteration

### Arrays as structured storage
Arrays let you represent a family of related values under one name, indexed by a subscript. In Enterprise PL/I, the key is that **bounds and types are part of the declaration**, and you should treat indexing as a correctness concern (off-by-one and invalid subscripts are classic defect sources).

### Counted loops (DO)
A counted `DO` loop gives you a controlled iteration variable and a clear range. The common beginner pattern is:
1. loop to populate values
2. loop to report values

This chapter’s exercise uses that exact structure because it teaches a clean separation between “build the data” and “display the data.”

---

### References (academic/official)
1. IBM, *Enterprise PL/I for z/OS Programming Guide* (language usage patterns, data and program structure). `https://www.ibm.com/docs/en/SSY2V3_6.1/pdf/pg.pdf`
theory.content:
Core Concepts in PL/I

After learning how single variables and simple output work, the next step is learning how PL/I handles repeated actions and groups of values. This chapter introduces three important ideas: arrays, loops, and basic control flow. These concepts are central to real programming because they let you do more than work with one value at a time.

The exercise in this chapter focuses on filling an array and displaying its contents with loops. That means the theory here is written to give you everything you need for that task while also introducing the wider idea of how PL/I controls the flow of a program.

What an Array Is

An array is a variable that can store multiple values under one name. Instead of declaring five separate variables, you can declare one array and access each element by its position. In PL/I, an array is declared by putting its size in parentheses, such as DCL ARR(5) FIXED BIN(31);.

This declaration creates five numeric elements: ARR(1), ARR(2), ARR(3), ARR(4), and ARR(5). Each one can store a value. Arrays are useful when you want to process a sequence of related data in a clean and organized way.

Using DO Loops

To repeat statements in PL/I, you use a DO loop. A counted loop has the form DO I = 1 TO 5;, which means that the statements inside the loop will run while I goes from 1 through 5. At the end of the loop body, you close it with END;.

In this chapter, the loop variable is usually something like I, declared as a numeric variable. Each time through the loop, you can use I as the array index. That makes loops and arrays work naturally together.

Filling and Displaying an Array

A common pattern in PL/I is to use one loop to assign values and another loop to display them. For example, inside a loop you might write ARR(I) = I * 2;. When I changes from 1 to 5, this stores the values 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 into the array elements.

After the array is filled, you can use a second loop with PUT SKIP LIST to display each value one by one. This makes the program easy to read because one part of the code prepares the data and the next part shows the results.

A Note on IF THEN ELSE

PL/I also supports decisions with IF THEN ELSE. This allows a program to choose different actions based on whether a condition is true. While you do not need it directly for this chapter's exercise, it is still part of the core foundation because arrays and loops are often combined with conditional logic in real programs.

Understanding that control flow includes both repetition and decisions helps you see the bigger picture of how a PL/I program works. In this chapter, though, your main focus should stay on arrays, indexing, loop counters, and printing values.

What You Need for the Exercise

  • Declare an array such as DCL ARR(5) FIXED BIN(31);
  • Declare a loop counter like I
  • Use DO I = 1 TO 5; to repeat statements
  • Assign values with ARR(I) = I * 2;
  • Use a second loop and PUT SKIP LIST to display each element

exercise.instructions: Create a PL/I program that fills an array of 5 numbers with the values 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 using a loop, then displays each value.
exercise.hint: Use an array declaration, one loop to fill the array, and a second loop to print the values.

questions.question: What is an array used for?
options: To store multiple values under one name

questions.question: Which PL/I structure is used to repeat statements?
What is the main purpose of IF THEN ELSE?
options: To make a decision based on a condition

questions.question: In ARR(3), what does the 3 represent?
Why are DO loops often used with arrays?
options: Because loops help process each element one by one
Because arrays only work inside IF statements

theory.content:
Advanced Topics in PL/I

This chapter introduces PL/I topics that feel more practical and closer to real applications. The main focus is string handling, because working with text is extremely common in business and system programming. You will also be introduced to related ideas such as structures and file I/O so that you see how string work fits into the wider PL/I environment.

The exercise for this chapter is still simple: store a piece of text, calculate its length, and display both the text and the result. The theory below gives you exactly what you need for that exercise while also explaining the broader subject around it.

Working with Character Data

In PL/I, text is commonly stored in variables declared with CHAR. For example, DCL STR CHAR(50); declares a character variable that can hold text up to a fixed size. You may also see VAR, as in DCL STR CHAR(50) VAR;, which allows the actual content length to vary up to the maximum size.

To assign a text value, you place the value in quotes, such as STR = 'HELLO, PL/I!';. This makes string variables easy to use for names, messages, labels, codes, and many other kinds of text data.

Using the LENGTH Function

One of the most useful built-in functions for strings is LENGTH. It returns the number of characters currently stored in a string. Because the result is numeric, it is usually stored in a numeric variable such as DCL LEN FIXED BIN(31);.

For example, LEN = LENGTH(STR); calculates the size of the text in STR and stores it in LEN. This is the key operation used in the chapter exercise, so it is important to understand that the function reads the current string value and returns its length as a number.

Other Useful String and Data Concepts

PL/I also supports string concatenation with ||, which joins text values together. For example, you can combine a first name and a last name into one larger string. This is not required for the exercise, but it is a natural next step after learning how to store and measure text.

Another important concept is the structure, which groups related fields together. PL/I is also widely used in programs that read and write files. Even though those ideas are not directly required for the exercise, they show why string handling matters so much: real programs often work with text records, messages, file data, and grouped information.

Displaying String Results

After storing the text and calculating its length, you display both values with PUT SKIP LIST. This lets you show the original string and the numeric length clearly. In this way, the program demonstrates both text processing and numeric output in one simple example.

This chapter therefore connects several important ideas: a character variable stores the text, a numeric variable stores the result of LENGTH, and PUT SKIP LIST displays everything to the user in a readable form.

What You Need for the Exercise

  • Declare a string variable with CHAR, often using VAR
  • Declare a numeric variable for the length result
  • Assign text using quotes
  • Use LENGTH(STR) to calculate the number of characters
  • Display both the text and the length with PUT SKIP LIST

exercise.instructions: Write a PL/I program that stores a short text in a character variable, calculates its length, and displays both the text and the length.
exercise.hint: Use CHAR(... ) VAR for the string, LENGTH for the size, and PUT SKIP LIST for output.

questions.question: Which type is commonly used to store text in PL/I?
What does the LENGTH function return?
options: The number of characters in a string
The number of loops in a program

questions.question: Which operator is used to join two strings?
What is a structure mainly used for?
What is the basic purpose of file I/O?
